coredata.dc:title: Functionalization of graphene quantum dots with antimorphine: Design of selective nanosensor for detection of morphine
coredata.dc:creator: Masteri-Farahani, Majid
Mosleh, Nazanin
coredata.dc:description: Surface functionalization of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with antimorphine produced a new turn-on fluorescent nanosensor for morphine detection. The antimorphine-GQDs were prepared through the covalent conjugation of GQDs with antimorphine. FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS) confirmed the conjugation of antimorphine to GQDs. Fluorescence intensity of the antimorphine-GQDs enhanced after addition of morphine allowing its sensitive determination with detection limit of 0.06 μM. Further experiments revealed that this nanosensor has no interaction with other narcotics such as methamphetamine and codeine. Thus, it can act as inexpensive and selective nanosensor for specification of low concentrations of morphine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Functionalization
Luminescence
Morphine
Sensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQDs functionalized with antimorphine (antimorphine-GQDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS full spectrum and high resolution spectra of antimorphine-GQDs.

(a) FT-IR spectra, (b) AFM image of antimorphine-GQDs (inset: the height profile along the drawn line in the image), (c) UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra (λexcitation = 360 nm) (inset: photographs of the GQDs and antimorphine-GQDs before (left) and during UV irradiation (right), (d) excitation wavelength dependence of fluorescence intensity of the antimorphine-GQDs.
Influence of adding narcotic drugs on the fluorescence intensity of antimorphine-GQDs. Insets show linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and concentration of narcotic drugs.

head.ce:title: Functionalization of graphene quantum dots with antimorphine: Design of selective nanosensor for detection of morphine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Tehran, Islamic Republic of Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface functionalization of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with antimorphine produced a new turn-on fluorescent nanosensor for morphine detection. The antimorphine-GQDs were prepared through the covalent conjugation of GQDs with antimorphine. FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS) confirmed the conjugation of antimorphine to GQDs. Fluorescence intensity of the antimorphine-GQDs enhanced after addition of morphine allowing its sensitive determination with detection limit of 0.06 μM. Further experiments revealed that this nanosensor has no interaction with other narcotics such as methamphetamine and codeine. Thus, it can act as inexpensive and selective nanosensor for specification of low concentrations of morphine.

ce:section.ce:para: Application of sensitive and easy procedures for quantification of various species is essential in chemical and biological studies [1,2]. Several attempts have been done to develop novel detection methods for improving the efficiency and sensitivity of biological sensors [3,4]. Recently, various nanostructured systems have been exploited for manufacturing low-cost and sensitive sensors [5–8]. Among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have become very important in scientific researches due to their outstanding properties [9,10]. In this regard, modification of GQDs has been emerged as efficient approach for achieving superior nanosensors for selective detection of chemical and biological molecules [11–13]. Morphine is the most common narcotic drug used by many people and thus its measurement is very important. Although various methods have been exploited for determination of morphine, they require time-consuming preparation procedures and expensive instruments [14–17]. Thus, some efforts have been done to attain simple, accurate, and inexpensive procedures to overcome these problems [18–20]. Here, we report an efficient nanosensor for detecting low concentrations of morphine. This system includes GQDs conjugated with anti-morphine to selectively detect morphine. To our knowledge, this is the first fluorescence investigation of interaction between a new conjugated antibody-GQDs and narcotic drugs. In comparison with common methods, the introduced nanosensor is simple and requires simple low-cost instrumentation for specification of morphine with high sensitivity.
The details of raw materials and instruments have been included in supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs functionalized with antimorphine (antimorphine-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of GQDs was done with modification of previous method [21]. First, citric acid (1 g) was heated to 200 °C in a beaker under static air. With melting the citric acid a yellowish color was appeared after some minutes. After 20 min, the color changed to orange indicating the formation of GQDs. The obtained GQD solution was cooled to room temperature and the pH was adjusted to 7 with 0.1 M NaOH. The solution was evaporated to achieve solid GQDs. Conjugation of the anti-morphine to GQDs was done by using 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (sulfo-NHS) as cross-linking agents. A solution of EDC (10 mg) in phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 1 mL, pH 7.4) was added into GQD solution (10 mg in 1 mL PBS). After 15 min stirring, a solution of sulfo-NHS (10 mg in 1 mL PBS) was added to the above mixture and stirred for 30 min. Finally, 10 μL of antimorphine solution (10 mg·mL−1 in 10 wt% bovine serum albumin, BSA) was added into the reaction mixture and stirred at room temperature for 2 h. The antimorphine-GQDs were precipitated with addition of ethanol and washed several times with ethanol to exclude the remaining EDC and sulfo-NHS reagents.

Firstly, aqueous solutions of morphine sulfate (0.66 mM), methamphetamine hydrochloride (2.5 mM) and codeine phosphate (2.5 mM) were prepared. 3 mL of antimorphine-GQDs solution (1 mg·mL−1 in PBS) was transferred into a quartz cuvette. Then, given volumes of the aqueous solutions of above mentioned narcotic drugs were added and after each addition, the fluorescence spectra of antimorphine-GQDs solutions were recorded.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) clearly demonstrated the presence of C, O, and N in antimorphine-GQDs (Fig. 1 ). Deconvolution of the N 1s peak revealed three peaks in the region of 399.3–400.6 eV due to the presence of nitrogen containing functional groups such as amide bond. Inspection of the C 1s region shows three peaks from 285 to 289 eV corresponding to the carbon atoms in three chemical states i.e. sp2 in CC, sp3 in C–N, C–O, and sp2 in CO [22]. The O 1s spectrum shows four peaks in region of 530–535 eV due to the presence of oxygen in different chemical environments i.e. C–OH, C–O–C, and CO. Such observations clearly verify the successful conjugation of antimorphine to the GQDs.

Fourier transform spectra of the prepared materials were acquired to monitor the stepwise functionalization of the GQDs (Fig. 2 a). The major FT-IR peaks of GQDs are seen at 3440 cm−1 due to stretching vibration of –OH groups as well as 1586 and 1398 cm−1 ascribed to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups, respectively. After activating the –COOH groups of GQDs by EDC/sulfo-NHS reagents, some new bands are appeared at 2850–2980 cm−1 due to the C–H stretching vibrations of incorporated sulfo-NHS ester groups. Functionalization of activated GQDs with antimorphine results in the appearance of two peaks at 1652 and 1579 cm−1 due to the formation of amide bonds and attachment of antimorphine to GQDs.

The surface morphology of the antimorphine-GQDs was investigated by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Fig. 2b). The height profile along the line drawn in AFM image illustrates that the height of antimorphine-GQDs is distributed in the range of 0.5–2.0 nm corresponding to more than one layer of graphene in this nanomaterial [22].

UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs and antimorphine-GQDs exhibited absorption peaks at around 360 nm due to the n → π* transitions of oxygen containing functional groups (Fig. 2c). Fluorescence spectra of both nanomaterials reveals narrow emission peak around 460 nm indicating a Stokes shift of 100 nm with respect to the applied excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 2c). It should be noted that after functionalization with antimorphine, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs decreased due to the quenching effect of antimorphine species. Furthermore, as can be seen in Fig. 2c (inset), after modification with antimorphine, the blue emission intensity of GQDs decreased in accordance to the results of the fluorescence spectroscopy. Further fluorescence study was also carried out in which the antimorphine-GQDs were excited under various wavelengths. As indicated in Fig. 2d, upon excitation with different wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm, the maximum fluorescence intensity is seen in 360 nm. Thus, in the next experiments, the excitation wavelength of 360 nm was used.

The fluorescence spectra of antimorphine-GQDs upon addition of narcotic drugs are shown in Fig. 3 . As can be seen, the increase in morphine concentration results in the successive increase in the fluorescence intensity of antimorphine-GQDs. The plot of F/F0 was depicted to examine the fluorescence enhancement as a function of the morphine concentration (insets of Fig. 3). It was found that the morphine concentration could be obtained from Stern–Volmer equation, F/F0 = 1 + K[M], where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of morphine, K is a constant, and [M] is the concentration of morphine, respectively. Interestingly, the plot of F/F0 versus morphine concentration is linear indicating the interaction of morphine with excited state of antimorphine-GQDs rather than ground state. On the other hand, the good linear relationship (r > 0.9977) allows the precise determination of morphine concentration in unknown samples. Unlike the clear changes in the case of morphine, the changes in the fluorescence intensities by increasing the concentration of methamphetamine and codeine are negligible. This is also clear in the plots of F/F0 versus methamphetamine and codeine concentrations as the obtained K values are ≈0. Consequently, the effect of morphine on the fluorescence of the antimorphine-GQDs was found to be different in comparison with other narcotics such as methamphetamine and codeine. With considering this effect, development of a selective and sensitive nanosensor for detection and determination of morphine is possible. The observed different behavior of the antimorphine-GQDs in the presence of different narcotics can be explained with considering their interactions with antimorphine receptor sites attached on the surface of GQDs. As seen in Fig. 1c, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs decreased after functionalization with antimorphine due to the Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) from the excited GQDs to the antimorphine. Only with addition of morphine and its bonding to the antimorphine receptor site, the FRET mechanism is blocked resulting in the fluorescence turning on of GQDs. The other narcotic drugs can not fit into the receptor sites of the antimorphine and thus have negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs.

In summary, covalent conjugation of antimorphine antibody to GQDs gives a new nanosensor with a receptor site that can selectively detect the target analyte i.e. morphine. The antimorphine receptor sites were attached to the GQDs through the two-step covalent bonding. The binding of the morphine to the receptor sites enhances the fluorescence intensity of the nanosensor. The prepared nanosensor has low detection limit (0.06 µM) for the measurement of morphine and provides a great promise for practical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of Kharazmi University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2019.01.057.
